---
title: "Chapter 3: The MNH Diagnostic Case Study"
subtitle: "Inference and Intervention | BK Advisors"
author: "BK Advisors"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    logo: bka-logo.png
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: slide
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#005CB9"
    hash-type: number
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
  warning: false
---

## Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

::: {.incremental}
- Apply the **iterative model-building process** used in program evaluations
- Start with a simple model and **expand it systematically** through interviews
- Identify **root nodes** (levers) vs. **intermediate nodes** in a causal system
- Use a causal model to **prioritize recommendations** and structure analysis
- Build a comprehensive MNH causal model through a simulated diagnostic case study
:::


## Chapter Overview

::: {.callout-box}
**The Book's Approach:** Chapter 3 of Ryall & Bramson presents the *Hubris Health* consulting case — a health insurance company whose renewals and new accounts are declining. Through iterative interviews, a causal model is built and refined.

**Our Adaptation:** We apply the same diagnostic methodology to a large-scale MNH program assessment — diagnosing why Country X's neonatal mortality program is underperforming.
:::

. . .

<div class="step-flow">
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #005CB9;">Case<br>Setup</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #83BD00;">Interview<br>Round 1</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #F8A623;">Interview<br>Round 2</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #FA7650;">Interview<br>Round 3</div>
<div class="step-arrow">→</div>
<div class="step-item" style="border-left: 4px solid #3E9B6E;">Final Model &<br>Recommendations</div>
</div>


# The Diagnostic Mindset {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">How technical advisors build causal models from stakeholder interviews</span>


## The Iterative Process

::: {.definition-box}
**Diagnostic Causal Modeling:** An iterative cycle of hypothesis → stakeholder interview → model revision → deeper questions → refinement.
:::

. . .

::: {.incremental}
- **Start simple:** Begin with the most stripped-down model possible
- **Walk back through causes:** What causes the outcome? What causes those causes?
- **Identify decision variables:** What can the program leadership actually *control*?
- **Sign the links:** Does increasing X increase or decrease Y?
- **Test with interviews:** Does the model match what stakeholders report?
- **Iterate:** Add complexity only when you actually need it
:::


## Key Principles from the Book

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box}
**1. Focus on Significant Drivers**

Not all variables matter equally. Identify the ones with the largest causal effects on the objective.
:::

::: {.callout-box .green}
**2. Seek Sources of Common Problems**

Look for common causes that explain multiple symptoms. These are high-leverage intervention points.
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .orange}
**3. Ask Specific Questions**

General questions yield vague answers. Use the model to generate *specific* hypotheses to test.
:::

::: {.callout-box .red}
**4. Bring It All Together**

The final model should tell a coherent causal story that links decisions to outcomes.
:::
:::
:::


# The Case Setup {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Country X's MNH program is underperforming</span>


## The Program Brief

::: {.callout-box}
**Requesting Body:** The program's leadership team

**Assessment:** Evaluate why Country X's MNH program has not achieved projected mortality reductions despite a substantial investment over 2 years.
:::

. . .

**Key Facts Provided:**

| Metric | Target (Year 2) | Actual (Year 2) |
|---|---|---|
| Neonatal Mortality Rate | On track | Significantly off target |
| Maternal Mortality Ratio | On track | Significantly off target |
| Intervention Coverage | 70% | 42% |
| Funds Disbursed | ~100% | ~96% |

. . .

::: {.callout-box .red}
**The puzzle:** The money has been spent, but outcomes haven't improved as projected. Why?
:::


## Initial Situational Assessment

Following the book's approach, start by mapping what we know:

. . .

**Walk back from the objective:**

::: {.incremental}
- What causes NMR reduction? → **Quality of care** during labor, delivery, and neonatal period
- What causes quality of care? → **Intervention coverage** (PPH bundle, CPAP, iKMC)
- What causes intervention coverage? → **Investment** in products, workforce, systems
- Investment has been made (nearly all funds disbursed) → So **something between investment and coverage is broken**
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Hypothesis:** The bottleneck is not in funding but in the *transmission mechanism* from investment to outcomes.
:::


## The Initial (Simple) Model

<table style="width: 90%; margin: 0.5em auto; font-size: 0.55em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect">Investment<br>(Disbursed)</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive">→ [+]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node">Programs<br>Implemented</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow positive">→ [+]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node">Intervention<br>Coverage</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;">
<span class="dag-arrow negative">→ [−]</span>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.6em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome">NMR</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

**Problems with this model:**

::: {.incremental}
- Too simple — treats "Programs Implemented" as a single black box
- Doesn't identify *which* programs are failing or *why*
- No confounders or common causes
- No decision variables beyond initial investment
:::

**We need interviews to deepen the model.**


# Interview Round 1: Ministry of Health {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">The supply-side view</span>


## MOH Director Interview

**Key quotes from the MOH Director of Maternal Health:**

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[Quote 1]{.example-label}

"We have procured the equipment — CPAP machines, PPH bundles, ultrasound devices. They're in the hospitals. But the staff don't know how to use them properly."
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[Quote 2]{.example-label}

"The training programs ran on schedule. Over 3,000 health workers attended workshops. But when I visit hospitals, I see the same old practices."
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[Quote 3]{.example-label}

"We have a severe shortage of midwives. The staff:patient ratio in neonatal units is 1:22, far worse than the 1:4 standard."
:::


## Updating the Model: Round 1

New variables discovered: **Equipment Availability**, **Training Delivery**, **Staff Competency**, **Staffing Levels**

<table style="width: 95%; margin: 0.4em auto; font-size: 0.48em;">
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.4em; vertical-align: middle;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect">Investment</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.4em; vertical-align: top;">
<div class="dag-node" style="margin-bottom: 0.4em;">Equipment<br>Procured ✓</div><br>
<div class="dag-node" style="margin-bottom: 0.4em;">Training<br>Delivered ✓</div><br>
<div class="dag-node confounder">Staffing<br>Levels ✗</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.4em;">
<div class="dag-node" style="margin-bottom: 0.4em;">Staff<br>Competency ✗</div><br>
<div class="dag-node">Equipment<br>Utilization ✗</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.4em;">
<div class="dag-node">Coverage</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.4em;">
<div class="dag-node outcome">NMR</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

::: {.callout-box}
**Key Insight:** Equipment was procured ✓ and training was delivered ✓, but **staff competency** and **equipment utilization** remain low ✗. The gap is between *inputs* and *effective use*.
:::


## What Questions to Ask Next?

The model suggests the bottleneck is in the **transmission** from inputs to utilization:

::: {.incremental}
- Why aren't trained health workers applying their training? → **Need to interview facility directors**
- Why is equipment procured but not used? → **Need to understand facility-level barriers**
- Is staffing the binding constraint? → **Need data on staff:patient ratios by facility**
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**The model guides the next interview.** Without it, we would be asking unfocused questions. The model generates *specific, testable hypotheses*.
:::


# Interview Round 2: Facility Directors {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">The front-line reality</span>


## Hospital Director Interviews

**Key findings from visiting 5 district hospitals:**

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[Finding 1: Staff Attrition]{.example-label}

"We trained 40 nurses on CPAP last year. Only 18 are still here. The rest transferred, emigrated, or left the profession." **Staff retention** is a critical missing variable.
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[Finding 2: Equipment Maintenance]{.example-label}

"The CPAP machines arrived 8 months ago. Two of our four units are already broken. We have no biomedical technician and spare parts take 3 months to arrive." **Equipment maintenance** breaks the chain.
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[Finding 3: Referral Failures]{.example-label}

"When a mother develops eclampsia at a health center, it takes 4-6 hours to arrange transport to our hospital. Many arrive too late." **Referral system gaps** are an independent cause of mortality.
:::


## Updating the Model: Round 2

New variables: **Staff Retention**, **Equipment Maintenance**, **Referral System**

<table style="width: 95%; margin: 0.3em auto; font-size: 0.42em;">
<tr>
<td colspan="7" style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node decision rect">Investment (Disbursed)</div>
</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;"><span class="dag-arrow">↓</span></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;"><span class="dag-arrow">↓</span></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;"><span class="dag-arrow">↓</span></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;"><span class="dag-arrow">↓</span></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node">Training<br>Delivered</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node">Staff<br>Competency</div>
</td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node">Equipment<br>Procured</div>
</td>
<td style="text-align: center;"><span class="dag-arrow">→</span></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node">Equipment<br>Utilization</div>
</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.2em;"><span class="dag-arrow">↑ ↓</span></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.2em;"><span class="dag-arrow">↑ ↓</span></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node confounder">Staff<br>Retention</div>
</td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td></td>
<td style="text-align: center; padding: 0.3em;">
<div class="dag-node confounder">Equipment<br>Maintenance</div>
</td>
</tr>
</table>

. . .

::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Critical discovery:** Staff retention and equipment maintenance are **common causes** (diverging triplets) that affect multiple downstream variables. These are high-leverage intervention points that the initial model completely missed.
:::


## Identifying Triplet Structures

The expanded model reveals all three triplet types from Chapter 2:

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box}
**Serial (Chain)**

Training → Competency → Coverage

*Conditioning on competency blocks the flow from training to coverage*
:::

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Diverging (Fork)**

Competency ← Retention → Utilization

*Staff retention is a common cause of both low competency (trained staff leave) and low utilization (not enough staff to operate equipment)*
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .orange}
**Converging (Collider)**

Staffing → Quality ← Equipment

*If we observe Quality is low AND Equipment is available, we can infer Staffing must be inadequate (explaining away)*
:::
:::
:::


# Interview Round 3: Community Health Workers {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">The demand side</span>


## Community Health Worker Interviews

**New perspective: the demand side of MNH services**

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[Finding 4: Transport Barriers]{.example-label}

"Many mothers in rural areas cannot reach the hospital in time. The nearest facility with a neonatal unit is 3 hours away by foot." **Geographic access** limits who benefits from improved facility quality.
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[Finding 5: Cultural Practices]{.example-label}

"Some families prefer traditional birth attendants. They only come to the hospital when complications are severe — by then it's often too late." **Health-seeking behavior** mediates the effect of facility quality.
:::

. . .

::: {.example-block}
[Finding 6: Out-of-Pocket Costs]{.example-label}

"Even though services are officially free, families pay for transport, food, and supplies during their stay. Many can't afford it." **Financial barriers** reduce effective demand.
:::


## Updating the Model: Round 3

Adding demand-side nodes: **Geographic Access**, **Health-Seeking Behavior**, **Financial Barriers**

. . .

The complete model now has **12 nodes** organized in three layers:

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box}
**Supply Side (from Rounds 1-2)**

- Investment → Training → Competency
- Investment → Equipment → Utilization
- Staff Retention affects both
- Equipment Maintenance affects utilization
- Referral System affects access
:::
:::

::: {.col}
::: {.callout-box .green}
**Demand Side (Round 3)**

- Geographic Access → Facility Births
- Health-Seeking Behavior → Facility Births
- Financial Barriers → Facility Births
- Facility Births × Quality → NMR
:::
:::
:::


## The Complete Causal Model

::: {.callout-box}
**The final model tells a coherent story:** Investment flows through three channels (people, products, systems), but is undermined by staff attrition, equipment breakdown, and referral failures on the supply side — and by geographic, cultural, and financial barriers on the demand side. Both supply and demand must work together to reduce NMR.
:::

. . .

| Layer | Nodes | Key Bottleneck |
|---|---|---|
| **Inputs** | Investment, Training, Equipment, Staffing | Adequate ✓ |
| **Transmission** | Competency, Utilization, Retention, Maintenance | **Broken** ✗ |
| **Demand** | Access, Behavior, Financial Barriers | **Underestimated** ✗ |
| **Outcomes** | Quality, Coverage, NMR | Below target ✗ |


# Model Comparison Across Rounds {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">How our understanding evolved</span>


## Evolution of the Model

| Round | Nodes | Key Addition | What We Learned |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial** | 4 | Investment → Programs → Coverage → NMR | Too simple; no mechanism |
| **Round 1** | 7 | Training, Equipment, Staffing, Competency | Supply-side decomposition |
| **Round 2** | 10 | Retention, Maintenance, Referral | Transmission failures |
| **Round 3** | 12 | Access, Behavior, Financial Barriers | Demand-side barriers |

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Key Insight from the Book:** Start with the simplest model and add complexity incrementally. At each stage, the model tells you *what questions to ask next*. This is the power of the diagnostic causal modeling approach.
:::


## Prioritizing Recommendations

Using the model to prioritize interventions:

. . .

::: {.columns-equal}
::: {.col}
**High Priority (Root causes with many downstream effects):**

::: {.callout-box .red}
<span class="step-number red">1</span> **Staff Retention Programs**

Root cause affecting both competency AND utilization. Invest in housing allowances, career paths, bonding agreements.
:::

::: {.callout-box .orange}
<span class="step-number orange">2</span> **Equipment Maintenance System**

Deploy biomedical technicians and establish spare parts supply chains. Equipment without maintenance is wasted investment.
:::
:::

::: {.col}
**Medium Priority:**

::: {.callout-box}
<span class="step-number">3</span> **Emergency Referral Network**

Independent pathway to mortality reduction. Partner with m-mama for transport coordination.
:::

::: {.callout-box .green}
<span class="step-number green">4</span> **Demand-Side Interventions**

Community health workers, transport subsidies, culturally-sensitive outreach to increase facility births.
:::
:::
:::


## Why Root Nodes Matter

::: {.definition-box}
**Root Node:** A node without any parents in the causal model. Root nodes are ultimate causes — intervening on them has the broadest downstream effects.
:::

. . .

In our model, the key root nodes are:

::: {.incremental}
- **Investment allocation** (decision variable — we control this)
- **Staff retention** (not currently addressed — new intervention needed)
- **Equipment maintenance** (not currently addressed — new intervention needed)
- **Geographic access** (structural constraint — requires referral systems)
:::

. . .

::: {.key-takeaway}
**The model reveals that the program invested in the right inputs** (training, equipment) **but missed critical transmission mechanisms** (retention, maintenance). Redirecting a portion of the budget to these mechanisms could unblock the entire chain.
:::


# R Workshop: Building Models Iteratively {background-color="#005CB9"}

<span style="color: rgba(255,255,255,0.7); font-size: 0.7em;">Using dagitty and ggdag to formalize each round</span>


## R: Round 1 — Simple Model

```{r}
#| label: round1-dag
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4

library(dagitty)
library(ggdag)
library(ggplot2)

# Round 1: Simple supply-side model
round1 <- dagitty('dag {
  Investment [pos="0,1"]
  Training [pos="1,0"]
  Equipment [pos="1,1"]
  Staffing [pos="1,2"]
  Competency [pos="2,0.5"]
  Utilization [pos="2,1.5"]
  Coverage [pos="3,1"]
  NMR [pos="4,1"]

  Investment -> Training
  Investment -> Equipment
  Investment -> Staffing
  Training -> Competency
  Staffing -> Competency
  Equipment -> Utilization
  Staffing -> Utilization
  Competency -> Coverage
  Utilization -> Coverage
  Coverage -> NMR
}')

ggdag(round1) + theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "Round 1: Supply-Side Model (7 nodes)")
```


## R: Round 2 — Adding Transmission Failures

```{r}
#| label: round2-dag
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

# Round 2: Add retention, maintenance, referral
round2 <- dagitty('dag {
  Investment [pos="0,1"]
  Training [pos="1,0"]
  Equipment [pos="1,2"]
  Staffing [pos="1,1"]
  Retention [pos="1.5,0.5"]
  Maintenance [pos="1.5,2.5"]
  Referral [pos="1.5,3"]
  Competency [pos="2,0.5"]
  Utilization [pos="2,2"]
  Coverage [pos="3,1"]
  NMR [pos="4,1"]

  Investment -> Training
  Investment -> Equipment
  Investment -> Staffing
  Investment -> Referral
  Training -> Competency
  Staffing -> Competency
  Retention -> Competency
  Retention -> Utilization
  Equipment -> Utilization
  Maintenance -> Utilization
  Competency -> Coverage
  Utilization -> Coverage
  Referral -> Coverage
  Coverage -> NMR
}')

ggdag(round2) + theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "Round 2: Adding Transmission Failures (10 nodes)")
```


## R: Round 3 — Complete Model

```{r}
#| label: round3-dag
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 6

# Round 3: Add demand-side variables
round3 <- dagitty('dag {
  Investment [pos="0,1.5"]
  Training [pos="1,0"]
  Equipment [pos="1,2"]
  Staffing [pos="1,1"]
  Retention [pos="1.5,0.5"]
  Maintenance [pos="1.5,2.5"]
  Referral [pos="1.5,3.5"]
  Competency [pos="2,0.5"]
  Utilization [pos="2,2"]
  Access [pos="2,3.5"]
  Behavior [pos="2,4.5"]
  Fin_Barriers [pos="2,5.5"]
  Facility_Births [pos="3,4"]
  Quality [pos="3,1.5"]
  NMR [pos="4,2.5"]

  Investment -> Training
  Investment -> Equipment
  Investment -> Staffing
  Investment -> Referral
  Training -> Competency
  Staffing -> Competency
  Retention -> Competency
  Retention -> Utilization
  Equipment -> Utilization
  Maintenance -> Utilization
  Competency -> Quality
  Utilization -> Quality
  Referral -> Access
  Access -> Facility_Births
  Behavior -> Facility_Births
  Fin_Barriers -> Facility_Births
  Quality -> NMR
  Facility_Births -> NMR
}')

ggdag(round3) + theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "Round 3: Complete Model (15 nodes)")
```


## R: Identifying What to Measure

```{r}
#| label: adjustment-analysis
#| echo: true

# What must we control for to estimate the causal effect
# of investment on NMR?
adjustmentSets(round3,
               exposure = "Investment",
               outcome = "NMR")

# What are the implied conditional independencies?
# These are testable predictions of the model
impliedConditionalIndependencies(round3)

# What are all paths from Investment to NMR?
paths(round3,
      from = "Investment",
      to = "NMR")
```

. . .

::: {.callout-box .green}
**Practical Value:** The `adjustmentSets()` function tells us which variables we *must* measure and control for in our evaluation. The `impliedConditionalIndependencies()` generate testable predictions — if the data violates them, our model is wrong.
:::


## Key Takeaways

::: {.key-takeaway}
**Build models iteratively.** Start simple and add complexity as interviews reveal new variables and mechanisms.

**Root nodes are leverage points.** Staff retention and equipment maintenance emerged as critical bottlenecks that the initial investment plan overlooked.

**The model guides the analysis.** Each round's model tells you what questions to ask next, what data to collect, and where the bottlenecks are.
:::


## Looking Ahead

::: {.callout-box}
**Next: Chapter 4 — Quantitative Causal Models**

We'll take the qualitative models built in this chapter and **add numbers**:

- Conditional probability tables (CPTs) for each node
- Joint probability distributions
- Bayes' rule for updating beliefs with evidence
- The mathematical foundation for precise inference and decision analysis
:::


##  {background-color="white"}

<br><br><br>

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
![](bka-logo.png){width="250px"}

<br>

[Inference and Intervention | Chapter 3: The MNH Diagnostic Case Study]{style="color: #005CB9; font-size: 0.6em;"}

[BK Advisors]{style="color: #666; font-size: 0.5em;"}
:::
